title: There's No Mystery to America's Fertility Decline
gist: IFS demographer Wendy Wang argues the U.S. fertility decline is not mysterious: 78% of the drop in the total fertility rate (2.1 in 2007 to 1.57 in 2025) is driven by a 38% collapse in births to women in their 20s, not by the sharper percentage drop in teen births that the New York Times highlighted; childlessness is near its 2006 record high, and the reproductive-technology gains that once offset it appear generational, not permanent.

insights.claim: On the same day, the Wall Street Journal's Louise Perry called falling U.S. birth rates 'a mystery' while the New York Times' Jessica Grose called it 'not a mystery,' attributing the decline mainly to the drop in teen births.
insights.speaker: Wendy Wang
insights.so_what: Frames the competing public narratives around fertility decline that Wang's demographic data is meant to settle — relevant to how the issue gets covered and politicized.
insights.quote: Falling birth rates are a mystery

insights.claim: Although teen fertility rates fell a steeper 72% since 2007 (42 to 12 births per 1,000 women ages 15-19) versus a 38% decline for women in their 20s, teens are not the main driver of the aggregate U.S. fertility decline because they contribute far fewer total births than women in their 20s.
insights.speaker: Wendy Wang
insights.so_what: Directly rebuts the NYT's teen-birth-decline explanation for falling fertility — analysts and policymakers should focus pronatalist attention on women in their 20s, not teen pregnancy trends.
insights.quote: Does that mean the decline in teen fertility rates is the main driver of the U.S. fertility? Absolutely not.
insights.locator: A drop by percentage can appear much more dramatic

themes.theme: The real driver: fertility collapse among women in their 20s

insights.claim: Between 2007 and 2025 the U.S. total fertility rate fell from replacement level (2.1 children per woman) to 1.57, and the drop in births among women ages 20-29 accounts for 78% of that overall decline.
insights.speaker: Wendy Wang
insights.so_what: Pinpoints the exact demographic segment responsible for most of the U.S. fertility decline, giving policymakers and pronatalist-focused investors a precise target population rather than a diffuse trend.
insights.quote: the drop in births among women ages 20 to 29 accounts for 78% of the overall decline in the total fertility rate.
insights.locator: Between 2007 and 2025, the U.S. total fertility rate

insights.claim: Fertility rates for women ages 20-24 were cut in half since 2007 (from 105 to 52 births per 1,000 women), the 25-29 rate fell about 28%, and the combined 20-29 fertility rate declined 38% over 18 years.
insights.speaker: Wendy Wang
insights.so_what: Quantifies the magnitude of the core decline for anyone modeling U.S. household formation, birth-linked consumer demand, or labor-force entry over the next two decades.
insights.quote: the fertility rate among women between ages 20 to 29 has declined 38% in the past 18 years.
insights.locator: The fertility rates for women ages 20 to 24

themes.theme: TFR vs. CFR: why the 'most women eventually have two kids' claim is stale

insights.claim: The common claim that 'most women eventually have two children' rests on the complete fertility rate (CFR), a retrospective measure only calculable after a generation finishes childbearing and therefore at least 20 years behind the current trend, unlike the real-time total fertility rate (TFR).
insights.speaker: Wendy Wang
insights.so_what: Warns against complacent demographic narratives built on lagging CFR data; forecasts of household formation and birth-linked demand should weight the more current TFR trend more heavily.
insights.quote: CFR Data is at least 20 years behind the current trend.
insights.locator: There is another measure demographers use

themes.theme: Record childlessness and technology's limited fix

insights.claim: By 2024, nearly 1 in 5 women (18.8%) reaching the end of their childbearing years had never given birth, approaching the record high set in 2006 and up from 16.5% just four years earlier in 2020.
insights.speaker: Wendy Wang
insights.so_what: Signals an accelerating shift toward permanent childlessness, relevant to long-run demand forecasts for housing, education, and family-oriented consumer sectors.
insights.quote: nearly 1 in 5 women reaching the end of their childbearing years (18.8%) had never given birth
insights.locator: Childlessness is Near a Record High

insights.claim: Childlessness among women ages 40-44 fell sharply from 20.4% in 2006 to 14.4% in 2016, plausibly linked to the American Society for Reproductive Medicine removing the 'experimental' label from egg freezing in 2012, which mainstreamed IVF and egg preservation.
insights.speaker: Wendy Wang
insights.so_what: A regulatory labeling change is presented as a causal lever that measurably raised late-in-life births — a case study for how fertility-tech policy shifts move demographic outcomes, relevant to fertility-tech investment theses.
insights.quote: in 2012, the American Society for Reproductive Medicine removed the “experimental” label from egg freezing
insights.locator: Some may argue that modern reproductive technology

insights.claim: The reproductive-technology-driven drop in childlessness benefited women born in the 1960s-70s (late Boomers and Gen-Xers); as early Millennials (born in the 1980s) now enter their 40s, childlessness is rising again, and Wang attributes this reversal to declining marriage rates among Millennials rather than to technology's limits.
insights.speaker: Wendy Wang
insights.so_what: Implies reproductive technology's fertility offset is cohort-limited, not a permanent fix — caution against extrapolating IVF-driven childlessness reversals onto younger, less-married generations.
insights.quote: Technology alone cannot explain this reversal.
insights.locator: However, women who benefited from this wave
